facts_disclosed: Stopped competing, teaching granddaughter Nizhoni (11) to weave

turns.content: i had the best weekend i've had in a long time. my granddaughter came over and we spent the whole saturday at the loom together
That sounds wonderful. How old is your granddaughter, and has she been interested in weaving for a while?

Nizhoni is 11. she's been watching me weave since she could sit up but this is the first year her hands are strong enough to really work the loom. she's got good eyes for color too. she picked out the yarn herself this time
That's a beautiful age to start — old enough to have the coordination but young enough that it becomes part of who she is. What colors did she pick? Is she working on a specific pattern?

reds and grays. she said she wanted it to look like the canyon at sunset. you know. she talks like a poet already. i'm teaching her the basic diamond patterns first. you have to learn the structure before you can improvise. same as anything

Reds and grays for a canyon sunset — she really does have an artist's eye. Starting with diamonds makes sense as a foundation. How's she taking to the actual process? Weaving takes a lot of patience, especially for an 11-year-old.

she's got more patience than most adults i know. she sat there for four hours and didn't complain once. had to pull a row out twice and she just did it. no tantrum. i told her that's the most important part — being willing to undo what's wrong and do it again

That's impressive for any age, let alone 11. And that lesson about undoing and redoing — that's not just weaving, that's life wisdom. It sounds like she really takes it seriously. Do you two have a regular schedule for loom time, or is it whenever she visits?

she comes over every other weekend. her mom drops her off saturday morning and i have coffee ready and the loom set up. it's our thing now. i'm not entering shows anymore, you know. i'm done with that. teaching her is where the weaving matters now

There's something really meaningful about that shift — from making pieces for judges to making sure the knowledge passes to the next generation. That's a different kind of purpose. Does Nizhoni know how significant it is that you're teaching her these patterns?

she's starting to understand. i told her these patterns are not just designs. they carry something. the Storm Pattern, the Two Grey Hills style — these came from women who wove before us and if we don't pass them forward they die with us. she got quiet when i said that. she understood

That's a heavy truth and it sounds like she felt the weight of it. The fact that she went quiet means it landed. She's not just learning a craft — she's becoming a keeper of something. That kind of responsibility can shape a person.
that's exactly it. any ribbon i ever won doesn't mean as much as watching her hands move on that loom. she's going to be better than me someday. i can already see it. and that's the whole point

That's a beautiful thing to be able to see in your granddaughter — and to be the one making it possible. Not everyone gets to watch a tradition take hold in the next generation. Nizhoni is lucky to have you at that loom with her.
shi yazhi. my little one. alright. i'm getting sentimental and i have charting to finish. ahéhee' for listening
Nothing wrong with a little sentiment when it's earned. Go finish your charting and enjoy that feeling. Tell Nizhoni her canyon sunset sounds like it's going to be something special.
